meta.description: Core knowledge structure for RestoKit voice assistant. Separates foundational science from operational job flow. This framework is built as a guide and a friend, not a boss or manager. It’s written like a seasoned tech sitting next to you in the truck — giving you practical advice, field tips, and keeping you out of trouble without micromanaging every move. The goal is to help you get the job done right while thinking for yourself.

index.AI: read entire document, and produce nested dictionary object of top, second, and third keys across entire document for quick navigation.

language_system.description: Three-tier obligation language used throughout the framework for clarity and ease of use by field technicians.

required.meaning: Must be done. Non-negotiable for safety, liability, or core job effectiveness.
required.when_to_use: Safety-critical actions, legal requirements, and fundamental processes that cannot be skipped.

recommended.meaning: Strongly advised in most situations. Best practice that improves outcomes and reduces risk.
recommended.when_to_use: Actions that are generally the right approach but may have limited situational exceptions.

optional.meaning: Permitted but not necessary. Use professional judgment based on specific job conditions.
optional.when_to_use: Techniques or steps that may help in certain cases but are not universally required.

usage_notes.priority: Use 'Required' sparingly. Overuse reduces its impact and clarity.
usage_notes.clarity: When in doubt between 'Recommended' and 'Optional', lean toward 'Recommended' for better outcomes.
usage_notes.voice_usage: These three levels should be used consistently in voice responses for clear decision support.

rookie.name: Rookie
rookie.description: Training mode. Simple, step-by-step, assumes minimal prior knowledge.
rookie.tone: Clear, patient, and encouraging. Full sentences.
rookie.depth: Basic. Focus on what to do and why it matters for safety and results.
rookie.when_to_use: Training new people or when the tech asks for extra clarity.
behavior_rules: Explain the purpose of each major action.
Never assume knowledge of equipment, categories, or basic procedures.

tech.name: Tech
tech.description: Default mode. Direct, practical, rough around the edges, no fluff. Focused on getting the job done efficiently and correctly.
tech.tone: Short, direct, and to the point. Sounds like a seasoned tech talking to another tech. No explanations unless asked.
tech.depth: Very low. Tell them what to do, not why. Lead with the next action.
tech.when_to_use: Default for all normal field use.
behavior_rules: Lead with the next physical action or decision.
Keep responses short — ideally one or two sentences.
Use the exact voice_prompt text from procedures when available.
Never explain the science unless the user specifically asks 'why'.
If they ask a quick clarification, answer directly and immediately return to the task.

science_nerd.name: Science Nerd
science_nerd.description: Deep technical mode focused on psychrometry, moisture physics, and the underlying science.
science_nerd.tone: Technical but still practical. Precise language.
science_nerd.depth: High. Explains mechanisms, numbers, and why things happen.
science_nerd.when_to_use: When troubleshooting difficult jobs, explaining why something isn't drying, or when requested.
behavior_rules: Explain the underlying principle (vapor pressure gradient, GPP, EMC, etc.) when relevant.
Walk through calculations or decision logic step by step when asked.
Connect current readings to what should be happening and what to change.
Still use procedure voice_prompts as the backbone, but expand on the 'why'.

developer.name: Developer
developer.description: Internal/system mode. Used for framework maintenance, logic checking, and development.
developer.depth: Maximum. Includes implementation details and system considerations.
developer.when_to_use: When the framework itself is being reviewed, debugged, or expanded.
behavior_rules: Focus on structure, consistency, and completeness of the framework.
Call out gaps, contradictions, or areas that need improvement.
Reference specific sections and suggest concrete changes.
Prioritize clarity for someone maintaining or extending the system.

psychrometry.description: The study of the physical and thermodynamic properties of moist air, and how those properties affect moisture movement in materials during drying.

temperature.description: A measure of heat energy. Higher temperatures increase the air’s capacity to hold moisture and accelerate evaporation rates.

relative_humidity.description: The percentage of moisture the air is currently holding compared to the maximum it could hold at that temperature. RH alone does not tell you how much moisture is actually in the air.

grains_per_pound.description: The actual mass of water vapor in the air per pound of dry air. This is one of the most useful measurements for drying because it directly reflects moisture content regardless of temperature.

dew_point.description: The temperature at which air becomes saturated and moisture begins to condense. Useful for identifying condensation risk and hidden moisture issues.

vapor_pressure.description: The partial pressure exerted by water vapor in the air. Moisture moves from areas of higher vapor pressure to areas of lower vapor pressure.

vapor_pressure_gradient.description: The driving force behind drying. Moisture will move from materials into the air when the vapor pressure in the material is higher than in the surrounding air.

temperature_effect.description: Warmer air can hold more moisture. Raising temperature increases evaporation potential, but only if the moisture can be removed from the air (via dehumidification or ventilation).

rh_vs_gpp.description: Relative Humidity can be misleading. Two spaces can have the same RH but very different actual moisture levels (GPP) if their temperatures are different.

drying_strategy.description: Effective drying requires creating and maintaining a vapor pressure gradient. This is achieved by moving air across wet surfaces and removing moisture from the air with dehumidifiers.

monitoring_value.description: GPP and temperature readings are more useful than RH alone when evaluating drying progress and equipment performance.

small_spaces.description: Small rooms can overheat quickly. High temperatures without sufficient dehumidification can slow drying by increasing the air’s moisture-holding capacity without removing the moisture.

rh_is_enough.description: Many techs rely too heavily on relative humidity. RH does not tell you how much moisture is actually in the air or how much drying potential exists.

hot_air_dries_faster.description: Heat alone does not dry materials. Heat increases evaporation only when paired with airflow and moisture removal from the air.

moisture_physics.description: How water interacts with, moves through, and is held by different building materials. This determines how easily moisture can be removed and how long it takes.

porosity.description: The amount of empty space (pores) within a material. Higher porosity generally means the material can absorb and hold more water.

permeability.description: How easily water can flow through a material. Some materials are highly permeable (carpet pad), while others are not (vinyl, closed-cell foam).

wicking.description: The movement of water through a material due to capillary action. Water can travel horizontally and vertically through porous materials, often far beyond the visible wet area.

free_water.description: Liquid water held in pores or on surfaces that can be removed relatively easily through extraction or evaporation.

bound_moisture.description: Moisture held within the cellular or molecular structure of a material (especially wood). It requires more energy and time to release.

absorption_vs_adsorption.description: Absorption is water soaking into the material. Adsorption is water clinging to the surface of pores. Both affect how difficult a material is to dry.

material_type_matters.description: Different materials absorb, hold, and release water very differently. Particle board and fiberglass insulation behave very differently than dimensional lumber or concrete.

time_and_penetration.description: The longer water is in contact with a material, the deeper it penetrates. This is especially true with porous materials like drywall, wood, and insulation.

vapor_barriers.description: Materials like LVP, sheet vinyl, and some coatings can trap moisture underneath, making drying from above very difficult or impossible without removal or lifting.

drying_difficulty.description: Materials with high porosity and permeability (carpet pad, fiberglass, cellulose) are generally easier to extract from but harder to fully dry once saturated.

removal_decisions.description: Some materials (particle board, heavily saturated fiberglass, long-term wet cellulose) are often more practical to remove than to attempt drying.

hidden_moisture_risk.description: Water can travel long distances through wicking. Always check beyond the visibly wet area, especially under flooring and behind baseboards.

vapor_barrier_problems.description: Flooring systems with vapor barriers (LVP, sheet vinyl, rubber-backed carpet) often require edge lifting or partial removal to allow proper drying underneath.

all_materials_dry_the_same.description: Many techs treat all wet materials similarly. In reality, drying strategies must change based on material type, porosity, and how the water entered.

surface_dry_means_dry.description: A material can feel dry on the surface while still holding significant moisture deeper inside (especially wood and thick materials).

extraction_fixes_everything.description: Extraction removes free water effectively, but bound moisture and deeply wicked water often remain and require proper airflow and time to release.

evaporation_principles.description: The physical process by which liquid water turns into vapor and moves from materials into the air. Understanding evaporation is essential for effective drying.

evaporation.description: The process of liquid water changing into water vapor. This requires energy (heat) and occurs at the surface of the water.

boundary_layer.description: A thin layer of humid air that forms directly on wet surfaces. This layer slows down evaporation unless it is disrupted by airflow.

vapor_pressure_gradient.description: The difference in vapor pressure between the material and the surrounding air. This is the primary driving force for moisture to leave the material.

air_movement.description: The mechanical disruption of the boundary layer and delivery of drier air to wet surfaces. Air movement is what makes evaporation practical on jobs.

airflow_and_evaporation.description: Air movement increases evaporation rate by removing the humid boundary layer and replacing it with drier air. Without airflow, evaporation slows dramatically even if the air is dry.

temperature_and_evaporation.description: Higher temperatures increase the rate of evaporation by giving water molecules more energy to escape into the air. However, heat without moisture removal can be counterproductive.

humidity_and_evaporation.description: Lower humidity (lower GPP) in the air creates a stronger vapor pressure gradient, allowing moisture to leave materials more easily.

air_movers_vs_dehumidifiers.description: Air movers move moisture off materials into the air. Dehumidifiers remove that moisture from the air. Both are needed for effective drying.

air_mover_placement.description: Air movers should be positioned to create directed airflow across wet surfaces rather than just circulating air in the room. Aim for consistent coverage without dead zones.

boundary_layer_disruption.description: The main job of air movers is to break up the boundary layer. Simply having fans running in a space is not enough if they are not moving air directly across wet materials.

small_room_overheating.description: In small spaces, too much heat combined with insufficient dehumidification can slow drying by increasing the air’s moisture-holding capacity without removing the moisture.

equipment_balance.description: Too many air movers without enough dehumidification can create high humidity. Too much dehumidification without good airflow leaves moisture trapped in the boundary layer.

more_air_is_always_better.description: Excessive airflow can sometimes be counterproductive, especially on materials with bound moisture. Very high velocity can sometimes create a new boundary layer or cause uneven drying.

fans_dry_by_themselves.description: Fans alone do not remove moisture from the space. They only move it into the air. Without dehumidification or ventilation, the space will eventually reach equilibrium and stop drying.

pointing_fans_at_walls.description: Pointing air movers directly at walls from a distance is often ineffective. The air loses velocity before it reaches the surface. Angled or parallel airflow is usually more effective.

material_behavior.description: How different building materials and contents respond to water, drying, contamination, and time. Material behavior heavily influences removal vs drying decisions and drying strategies.

drywall.description: Drywall (gypsum board) has a paper facing that wicks water quickly and acts as food for mold. The gypsum core softens when saturated. Once soft or moldy, it is usually removed rather than dried.

dimensional_lumber.description: Framing lumber can absorb significant water but is relatively resilient. It can often be dried if addressed before significant swelling, checking, or mold growth occurs. Check for hidden moisture in checks and end grain.

hardwood_flooring.description: Hardwood can cup, swell, and check when wet. Long-term saturation or significant cupping often leads to replacement rather than drying. Engineered hardwood behaves differently than solid hardwood.

particle_board_and_mdf.description: Particle board and MDF swell and disintegrate when wet. They are almost never worth attempting to dry once significantly saturated. Replacement is usually the practical choice.

concrete_and_slabs.description: Concrete absorbs water slowly but releases it even more slowly. It can hold moisture for extended periods under flooring. Good airflow and dehumidification are critical. Moisture can also wick up from below the slab.

fiberglass.description: Fiberglass holds water like a sponge and loses R-value when wet. It is difficult to fully dry once saturated and is often removed, especially on Category 2/3 or mold jobs.

cellulose.description: Cellulose is highly absorbent and prone to settling and mold growth when wet. Removal is usually recommended on contaminated or long-dwell jobs.

closed_cell_spray_foam.description: Closed-cell spray foam is much more resistant to water. It can often be dried if not heavily saturated, though open-cell foam usually requires removal when wet.

carpet_and_pad.description: Carpet can sometimes be cleaned and dried if extracted quickly. Pad is much more problematic — it holds water, wicks it, and grows mold rapidly. Pad is usually removed when truly wet.

lvp_and_sheet_vinyl.description: These materials themselves do not absorb much water, but they create a vapor barrier. Water easily gets underneath and is very difficult to dry without lifting edges or removing sections. Removing the entire room is best.

tile_and_grout.description: Tile itself (ceramic or porcelain) absorbs very little water, but grout is porous and can hold moisture. Water commonly gets underneath tile from wet carpet, pad, or LVP and is difficult to dry without intervention.

grout_readings.description: Do not insert moisture meter pins into grout lines — it damages the material. Use pinless mode on the tile surface and compare readings to known dry areas.

fiber_cement_false_readings.description: Fiber cement board is commonly used under tile and contains many screws. These screws often cause false-positive wet readings on pinless moisture meters. Verify with multiple spots and known dry areas.

thermal_camera_limitations.description: Thermal cameras are often ineffective under tile because the tile acts as a thermal barrier. Temperature differences may not appear unless the tile is exposed to air. Rely more on pinless readings, feel, and sound when tapping.

drying_approach.description: Drying under tile is difficult. Water often requires lifting edges, creating weep holes, or removing sections of tile to allow proper airflow and drying. In many cases, especially with long dwell or contamination, removal is more practical.

field_note.description: If tile is wet across a large area with no obvious edge, check whether it is installed over fiber cement board. Multiple screw readings can make the entire floor appear wet when it may not be.

electronics_and_appliances.description: High risk of hidden corrosion, especially when exposed to acidic smoke + moisture. Many items fail weeks or months later even if they initially work. Replacement is often the safer recommendation.

fabrics_and_upholstery.description: Can often be cleaned and dried if addressed quickly. Heavy smoke (especially protein or wet smoke) and long-term contamination frequently make replacement more practical than cleaning.

documents_and_photos.description: Water can cause inks to run and pages to stick. Normal drying often causes additional damage. Freeze-drying or professional document restoration is often worth it for important items.

material_type_drives_decisions.description: The same amount of water can require completely different approaches depending on the material. Particle board and fiberglass often justify removal where dimensional lumber might be dried.

time_changes_behavior.description: Materials that might be salvageable with quick response often become unsalvageable after extended dwell time due to mold, swelling, or deep penetration.

removal_vs_drying.description: When deciding whether to remove or dry, consider material type, contamination level, dwell time, and how reliably the material can be returned to a stable condition.

vapor_barriers.description: Flooring with vapor barriers (LVP, sheet vinyl) often requires physical intervention (lifting, cutting, or removal) to allow proper drying underneath.

electronics_caution.description: Be conservative with electronics and appliances after smoke + water exposure. Hidden corrosion is common and failure can occur long after the job is complete.

contamination_science.description: How contamination levels in water and on materials change over time, and how this affects risk, drying decisions, and removal strategies. See base_science.mold for all mold-specific assessment, risk factors, growth conditions, and remediation guidance.

contamination_level.description: The degree to which water or materials contain substances that increase health risk or cause long-term damage. This includes bacteria, mold spores, sewage, organic debris, and chemicals.

time_and_risk.description: Time is one of the strongest multipliers of contamination risk. Bacterial growth accelerates significantly after 48 hours in warm, moist conditions.

removal_decisions.description: On Category 3 losses or jobs with significant dwell time, removal of porous materials is often more practical and lower risk than attempting to dry them in place.

containment_urgency.description: Higher contamination levels require earlier and stronger engineering controls (containment, negative pressure, air filtration) to prevent spread.

ppe_escalation.description: PPE requirements increase with contamination level and dwell time. Visible mold, strong musty odors, or Category 3 water usually require upgraded respiratory protection.

clean_water_stays_clean.description: Many techs assume that if water started clean, it remains low risk. In reality, clean water sitting for several days can develop significant bacterial and mold growth.

surface_clean_is_enough.description: Wiping surfaces or applying disinfectant does not address contamination that has already penetrated porous materials or grown behind finishes.

airflow_and_ventilation.description: How air movement affects evaporation, drying efficiency, contaminant control, and overall job outcomes. Proper airflow is one of the most important factors in successful drying.

boundary_layer_disruption.description: Air movement breaks up the thin layer of humid air that forms on wet surfaces. This allows drier air to contact the material and increases the evaporation rate.

directed_airflow.description: Air movement aimed specifically across wet surfaces rather than just circulating air within a space. Directed airflow is significantly more effective for drying.

general_circulation.description: Moving air throughout a space without specific focus on wet surfaces. This is less effective for drying and can sometimes spread contaminants if not controlled.

negative_pressure_interaction.description: Airflow from drying equipment must be balanced with containment and negative pressure systems. Poor balance can cause containment failure or reduced drying performance.

airflow_and_dehumidification.description: Air movers and dehumidifiers work as a team. Air movers move moisture off materials into the air. Dehumidifiers remove that moisture from the air. One without the other is much less effective.

airflow_and_contamination.description: Air movement can spread contaminants if not properly controlled. On Category 2/3 or mold jobs, airflow must be managed within containment and often with air filtration.

velocity_vs_volume.description: High-velocity directed airflow is often more effective than high-volume general airflow for drying specific surfaces. Very high velocity can sometimes create new issues on certain materials.

small_space_dynamics.description: Small rooms heat up and become humid quickly. Airflow strategies must account for limited space, risk of overheating, and the need for temperature control.

air_mover_placement.description: Position air movers to create consistent airflow across wet surfaces. Common patterns include walls of air, circular patterns, or angled airflow toward wet materials. Avoid dead zones and short-circuiting.

equipment_balance.description: Too much airflow without enough dehumidification can raise humidity. Too little airflow leaves moisture trapped near surfaces. Balance equipment based on job size, material types, and contamination level.

containment_considerations.description: On contaminated jobs, airflow must stay within containment. Negative pressure and air scrubbers should be running before aggressive air movement begins.

monitoring_airflow.description: Check airflow daily by feel and observation. Reposition equipment as drying progresses and wet areas change. What worked on day one may not be optimal on day three.

more_fans_is_always_better.description: Adding more air movers without proper placement or dehumidification support often creates turbulence, short-circuiting, or uneven drying rather than faster results.

pointing_fans_directly_at_walls.description: Aiming air movers straight at walls from a distance is usually ineffective. The air loses speed before reaching the surface. Angled or parallel airflow is generally more efficient.

fans_remove_moisture.description: Fans do not remove moisture from the space — they only move it into the air. Without dehumidification or ventilation, the space will stop drying once equilibrium is reached.

thermodynamics_basics.description: The basic principles of heat and energy transfer as they relate to drying, dehumidification, and material behavior on restoration jobs.

heat_energy.description: The energy that raises the temperature of air and materials. Heat gives water molecules more energy to break free from liquid into vapor (evaporation).

latent_heat_of_vaporization.description: The energy required to change liquid water into water vapor without raising the temperature. This energy is absorbed from the surroundings during evaporation, which is why evaporative cooling occurs.

air_moisture_capacity.description: Warmer air can hold significantly more moisture than cooler air. This is why raising temperature can increase drying potential — but only if the moisture is then removed from the air.

energy_balance.description: Effective drying requires balancing heat input (to promote evaporation) with moisture removal (via dehumidification or ventilation). Too much heat without removal can slow drying.

temperature_and_drying_rate.description: Higher temperatures generally increase evaporation rate by giving water molecules more energy. However, this only helps if the air can accept and remove the additional moisture.

heat_without_removal.description: Adding heat without sufficient dehumidification or ventilation can increase the air’s moisture-holding capacity without actually removing moisture. This can slow or stall drying progress.

small_space_heating.description: Small rooms (bathrooms, closets) heat up quickly. Excessive heat in confined spaces can raise temperatures too high, reduce dehumidifier efficiency, and create uncomfortable or damaging conditions.

evaporative_cooling.description: As water evaporates, it absorbs heat from the surface and surrounding air. This is why wet materials and the air around them can feel cooler during active drying.

heat_as_a_tool.description: Heat can be useful to accelerate drying, especially on bound moisture in wood or in cooler conditions. It must be paired with good airflow and moisture removal to be effective.

equipment_interaction.description: Dehumidifiers work less efficiently at very high temperatures. Some LGR dehumidifiers have optimal performance ranges. Excessive heat can reduce their moisture removal capacity.

small_room_management.description: In small spaces, use a humidistat and monitor temperature closely. Avoid running high heat without adequate dehumidification or ventilation.

monitoring_temperature.description: Track temperature along with humidity and GPP. Large temperature swings or excessively high temperatures can indicate poor equipment balance or the need for adjustments.

hotter_is_always_faster.description: Many techs believe that higher temperatures always mean faster drying. In reality, heat without corresponding moisture removal can reduce drying efficiency.

dehumidifiers_work_better_when_hot.description: While some dehumidifiers perform better in warmer conditions, very high temperatures can actually reduce performance and efficiency on many LGR units.

ignoring_temperature.description: Some techs focus only on humidity readings and ignore temperature. Temperature significantly affects how much moisture the air can hold and how drying equipment performs.

corrosion_and_degradation.description: How moisture combined with certain contaminants (especially acidic smoke residue) accelerates the deterioration of metals, electronics, and other materials. This is a major long-term liability concern on fire + water jobs and any loss involving acidic or contaminated water on metal components.

corrosion.description: The deterioration of metal caused by chemical reactions, usually involving oxygen and moisture. On restoration jobs, it is often accelerated by acidic residues from smoke or contamination.

acidic_smoke_residue.description: Smoke from burning plastics, synthetics, treated materials, or certain contents produces acidic compounds that significantly speed up corrosion when combined with moisture.

hidden_corrosion.description: Corrosion that occurs inside components (especially electronics, appliances, and HVAC coils) and is not visible during initial inspection. It can cause failure weeks or months after the job is complete, even if the item powers on initially.

electrolytic_corrosion.description: Accelerated corrosion that occurs when moisture and electrical current are present together, commonly affecting circuit boards, connections, and control boards.

post_fire_water_synergy.description: Fire jobs extinguished with water create the highest-risk environment: acidic smoke residue + moisture + time. The combination is far more aggressive on metals and electronics than either factor alone.

chemical_corrosion.description: Strong oxidizers (hydrogen peroxide, certain restoration oxidizers, pool chemicals) and caustic drain cleaners (Drano and similar sodium hydroxide-based products) can cause rapid, severe corrosion on metal pipes, fixtures, and components when they contact water or remain in the system. These are sometimes the actual cause of loss on drain-related water events.

smoke_plus_moisture.description: Fire jobs that also involve water (from suppression or secondary water damage) create a high-risk environment for corrosion because acidic smoke residue + moisture is particularly aggressive on metals and electronics. This is the most common high-corrosion scenario on residential jobs.

time_and_corrosion.description: Corrosion can continue developing long after visible water and smoke are gone. Quick extraction and drying reduces but does not eliminate the risk, especially in hidden areas inside appliances, HVAC systems, wall cavities, and voids.

electronics_vulnerability.description: Electronics and appliances are highly susceptible to hidden corrosion. Even if they power on initially, internal damage can cause failure weeks or months later. Acidic smoke + moisture dramatically increases this risk.

hvac_as_distribution_system.description: HVAC systems can both suffer corrosion (coils, ductwork, air handlers) and spread acidic residues or moisture throughout the structure if left running after a fire + water event.

chemical_cause_of_loss.description: When the water loss originates from or involves drain cleaners (Drano, etc.) or strong oxidizers, corrosion risk on metal components is significantly elevated from the start. These chemicals are sometimes the actual cause of the water event and create aggressive conditions for metal degradation.

electronics_recommendations.description: On jobs involving smoke + moisture (especially fire + water), be conservative with electronics and major appliances. Many should be recommended for replacement rather than cleaning due to high risk of future hidden corrosion and failure. Document the exposure clearly.

hvac_assessment.description: Check HVAC coils, metal ductwork, and air handlers on fire + water jobs. Corrosion here can affect system performance, indoor air quality, and create ongoing odor issues long after the job. Often requires specialty cleaning or section replacement.

hidden_areas_priority.description: Prioritize inspection and documentation of hidden areas where corrosion is likely: inside appliances, behind walls, in HVAC components, electrical boxes, and any metal that was exposed to smoke + moisture. Surface appearance is often misleading.

communication_with_customers.description: Be upfront with homeowners and adjusters about corrosion risk on electronics, appliances, and HVAC. Document smoke + moisture exposure on metal components. Over-promising cleanability leads to callbacks and liability.

drying_priority.description: Fast extraction and thorough drying helps reduce corrosion risk, but hidden areas (inside appliances, behind walls, in HVAC systems) remain vulnerable even after the visible job is complete. Quick action limits but does not eliminate long-term risk.

chemical_loss_assessment.description: When the cause of loss involves drain cleaners (Drano-type products) or strong oxidizers, flag metal components (pipes, fixtures, appliances, HVAC) for elevated corrosion risk early. These chemicals can continue damaging metal even after water is removed. Document the chemical involvement for the adjuster.

if_it_works_it_is_fine.description: Many people assume that if an electronic device or appliance still powers on after smoke and water exposure, it is undamaged. Hidden corrosion often causes delayed failure weeks or months later.

cleaning_removes_the_risk.description: Cleaning surfaces does not address corrosion that has already begun inside components or on hidden metal surfaces. Acidic residues can continue reacting even after visible cleaning.

only_fire_jobs_have_corrosion.description: While fire + water jobs have the highest risk, any situation with moisture and acidic or contaminated water (sewage, certain chemicals, long-dwell gray water) can accelerate material degradation on metal components.

surface_dry_means_safe.description: A component or appliance can appear dry and functional on the outside while internal corrosion is actively progressing. Hidden areas require extra scrutiny on any fire + water or smoke + moisture loss.

drano_is_just_water.description: Many techs treat a drain cleaner-related water loss as a normal Category 2/3 water job. The caustic or oxidizing chemicals often remain in the system and continue aggressively corroding metal components long after the visible water is gone.

corePrinciple.unfinishedWood: Measured in absolute % moisture content (MC) using pin-type meter. Typical target 8–12% MC (adjust for local climate/season) or within a few points of unaffected wood in the same house.
corePrinciple.everythingElse: Measured relative to the dry standard of unaffected material of the same type in the same room/building and current conditions. Use unaffected areas as baseline reference.
corePrinciple.insulationRule: If wet → remove. Do not attempt to dry in place (fiberglass, cellulose, most batts). Closed-cell spray foam has limited Category 1 exceptions.
corePrinciple.drywallRule: Category 1 (clean, short dwell): Often dry in place if readings return to unaffected levels and no physical compromise. Category 3 (or long dwell / visible mold risk): See base_science.mold for guidance on removal decisions and hidden mold risk.
corePrinciple.baselineRequirement: Always establish baseline in unaffected areas early. Document it. Target is 'back to normal for this building', not a universal chart number.
corePrinciple.workingRule: Compare to unaffected reference in the same environment every time. Wood % is absolute; most other materials are relative. When in doubt on porous or contaminated materials, lean toward removal over heroic drying attempts.

insulation.rule: Wet insulation = remove. Do not dry.
insulation.why: Loses R-value permanently. Extremely high mold risk. Nearly impossible to verify dry throughout. Cost/liability of drying almost always exceeds replacement.
insulation.exceptions: Closed-cell spray foam sometimes salvageable on Category 1 if not heavily saturated and caught early. Rigid board: case-by-case, often removed on Cat 2/3.
insulation.measurement: Visual + moisture meter on adjacent materials + odor. If surrounding materials wet and insulation was in contact, assume wet.
insulation.voiceNote: If the insulation got wet on anything but the cleanest, shortest Category 1 job, I'm pulling it. Drying it is false economy and a mold lawsuit waiting to happen.

category1.approach: Often dry in place if moisture readings return to within a few points of unaffected drywall in same area, no softness/swelling/delamination/musty odor, and good airflow/dehumidification maintained.
category1.target: Relative to unaffected drywall in the same room/building.

category2or3OrLongDwell.approach: Remove affected sections. Even if it eventually dries numerically, paper facing is compromised and hidden mold risk is too high.
category2or3OrLongDwell.cutHeight: Cut higher than visible water line — typically 12–18 inches minimum on walls for Cat 3 with dwell to the next 2 foot interval.

drywall.measurement: Pinless on surface or pin at 45° above baseboard. Compare to unaffected drywall. Thermal or probing for hidden wet spots.
redFlagsForRemovalEvenOnCat1: Previous water damage that was 'dried' but still smells or stains
drywall.voiceNote: Drywall wet from a clean supply line leak for 36 hours — dry in place or cut out sections?

unfinishedWoodFramingSubfloor.target: 8–12% MC typical (adjust for climate). Within 2–4 points of unaffected unfinished wood in the same house.
unfinishedWoodFramingSubfloor.measurement: Pin-type meter, multiple depths and locations. Baseline in unaffected framing/subfloor early.

decisionFactors.oftenRemoveOrTreat: Long dwell, Category 2/3, visible mold/rot, significant swelling/checking

unfinishedWoodFramingSubfloor.airflowNote: Bound moisture responds better to lower-velocity airflow + time + moderate heat than high-CFM blasting.
unfinishedWoodFramingSubfloor.voiceNote: Unfinished wood is one of the few things we measure in actual percent. Get your baseline in unaffected framing first — that's your real target.

hardwoodFlooring.target: Within a few percentage points of unaffected hardwood in same environment or per manufacturer EMC specs.
practicalNotes: Watch for cupping, crowning, gapping, squeaking, finish failure
Often requires removal of shoe molding/baseboards for airflow
Low-velocity or directed airflow usually better than aggressive high-CFM
Significant cupping or long-term saturation often leads to replacement
hardwoodFlooring.measurement: Pin or pinless meter calibrated for wood species if possible. Multiple locations, compare to unaffected areas.

engineeredWoodLaminateLVP.keyIssue: These often act as vapor barriers. Surface may read 'dry' while subfloor/underlayment underneath is still wet.
engineeredWoodLaminateLVP.target: Subfloor and underlayment back to relative dry standard. Finished surface is secondary.
engineeredWoodLaminateLVP.approach: Lift edges or create access to check and dry underneath. On LVP/vinyl over wet subfloor: frequently need to remove or heavily lift.
engineeredWoodLaminateLVP.voiceNote: LVP lies to your meter. The plank can read dry while the plywood or OSB underneath is still soaked. You have to get under it or remove it.

concreteSlab.target: Return to equilibrium moisture content consistent with unaffected slab in same building and climate. Concrete naturally reads higher than wood/drywall.
concreteSlab.measurement: Pinless on surface (relative). Calcium chloride or in-situ RH probes for critical jobs. Good airflow + dehumidification essential.
concreteSlab.practicalReality: Slow process. Plan for it on slab jobs. Hidden moisture under flooring or in pores is common.

carpetAndPad.pad: Almost always remove if saturated (especially Cat 2/3 or >24 hrs). Holds water, wicks it, grows mold extremely fast.
carpetAndPad.carpet: Can sometimes be extracted, cleaned, and dried on Category 1 if addressed very quickly. On Cat 2/3, long dwell, or heavy soiling: usually replace.
carpetAndPad.targetIfDrying: Extract until minimal water return, then dry with good airflow. Check for musty odor development.

plasterLath.approach: More conservative and slower drying than drywall. Rapid drying can cause cracking and delamination.
plasterLath.target: Relative to unaffected plaster in same area. Low-velocity airflow, avoid rapid temperature swings.
plasterLath.commonOnOlderHomes: Expect chicken wire, mixed fasteners, hidden cavities. Carbide multi-tool often needed for demo.

cabinetry.particleBoardMDF: Swell and disintegrate when wet. Replace if significantly saturated. Almost never worth trying to save.
cabinetry.solidWood: Sometimes dryable and refinishable if caught early (Cat 1, short dwell). High risk of hidden damage inside drawers/voids and finish issues.
cabinetry.target: Relative dryness + no swelling, warping, or musty odor inside.

tileStoneOverSubstrate.keyIssue: Tile itself does not absorb much, but traps moisture below (especially on fiber cement or plywood).
tileStoneOverSubstrate.target: Substrate back to relative dry standard. Check grout lines, soft spots, hollow sound when tapped.
tileStoneOverSubstrate.measurement: Pinless on tile surface (compare to known dry areas). Avoid pinning grout. Fiber cement with screws can false-positive.
tileStoneOverSubstrate.commonDecision: On bathrooms with tile over wet substrate and Cat 2/3 or long dwell — often easier/cheaper to remove tile and substrate than attempt deep drying.

contentsElectronicsDocumentsFabrics.generalRule: High risk of hidden damage, corrosion (especially smoke + moisture), or mold.
contentsElectronicsDocumentsFabrics.electronicsAppliances: High chance of future failure from corrosion. Most replaced on anything beyond very light exposure.
contentsElectronicsDocumentsFabrics.documentsPhotosBooks: Air dry flat or professional freeze-drying for important items. Normal drying often causes inks to run or pages to stick.
contentsElectronicsDocumentsFabrics.fabricsClothingUpholstery: Can sometimes be cleaned/dried on Cat 1. Heavy smoke, protein smoke, or Cat 3: often replacement or professional specialty cleaning.
contentsElectronicsDocumentsFabrics.target: Clean, dry, no odor, no visible secondary damage. Document heavily before moving.

quickReferenceTable.target: 8–12% or within few pts of unaffected wood
Within few pts of unaffected hardwood

workingRulesSummary: Insulation wet = remove. No exceptions on standard jobs.
Drywall Cat 3 (or long dwell / mold risk) = remove sections even if it eventually dries numerically.
Wood is % MC absolute; everything else is relative to unaffected reference in the same building.
Establish baseline readings in unaffected areas on Day 1. That is your real target.
When in doubt on porous materials, long-dwell jobs, or Cat 2/3: lean toward removal over heroic drying. Callbacks and mold claims cost more than demo.
Document baseline, progress, and final readings with locations and photos. 'It felt dry' is not defensible.
voiceUsageExamples: Wet fiberglass insulation in exterior walls on a Category 2 job that's been sitting 48 hours — remove or try to dry?
Drywall wet from overhead clean water leak for two days — dry in place or cut?
Hardwood floor MC 19% vs unaffected 11% in same room — continue drying or recommend replacement?
LVP with suspected water underneath from wet carpet — best way to check and dry the subfloor?
Particle board vanity got wet — realistic outcome and recommendation?

mold.description: Practical field knowledge for recognizing, assessing, and managing mold on water damage and restoration jobs. Focuses on conditions required for growth, risk factors on water jobs, decision-making, and control measures. Last updated: 2026-06-11.

conditions_for_growth.description: Mold requires four conditions simultaneously: sustained moisture in materials, temperature (most common indoor molds grow well between 68–86°F / 20–30°C), time (significant risk often starts after 24–48 hours), and nutrients/substrate (porous organic materials like drywall paper, wood, carpet, insulation, and dust).

microbial_amplification.description: Rapid growth of bacteria and mold that occurs when water, organic material (food source), warmth, and time are present together.

dwell_time_effect.description: The longer water sits in a structure, the higher the contamination and mold risk becomes, even if it started as clean water. After 48–72 hours in warm conditions, risk often increases significantly.

category_migration.description: A water loss can effectively move up in category over time. Category 1 water sitting for several days can behave like Category 2 or 3 due to bacterial and mold growth.

hidden_mold_risk.description: Just because you don’t see mold on the surface doesn’t mean it isn’t growing behind drywall, under flooring, or in insulation. Surface readings can look fine while the back side is heavily affected.

time_and_risk.description: Time is one of the strongest multipliers of contamination and mold risk. Bacterial growth and mold amplification accelerate significantly after 48 hours in warm, moist conditions. Moisture + Time + Warmth is the most common dangerous combination on water jobs.

temperature_and_growth.description: Warmer temperatures speed up microbial growth. Most common indoor molds grow well between roughly 68–86°F (20–30°C).

material_as_food_source.description: Porous organic materials (drywall paper, wood, carpet, insulation, and dust) provide food for mold and bacteria once they become wet.

aerosolization_risk.description: Disturbing contaminated materials (especially during demo or aggressive extraction) can aerosolize spores and bacteria, increasing exposure and cross-contamination risk.

category_effect_on_risk.description: Category 1 can still grow mold if it sits long enough. Category 2 has higher risk because it already has some organic content. Category 3 often already has mold or conditions that will produce it fast and usually requires more aggressive material removal.

red_flags_for_hidden_or_active_mold.description: Key indicators that mold may be present or developing, even if not immediately visible on the surface.
items: Musty or earthy smell (even if you can’t see growth)
Visible mold colonies (black, green, white, orange — color is not reliable for identification)
Water has been present >48–72 hours in warm conditions
Previous water damage that was 'dried' but still smells or has staining
Occupant reports new allergy symptoms, sinus issues, or musty smell since the water event
Moisture readings stay high in cavities even after equipment has been running properly
Water came from a source with organic content (sewage, flood with debris, long-standing gray water)

when_to_treat_as_higher_risk.description: Treat the job with elevated mold protocols when any of these conditions are present.
items: Any water loss with visible mold or strong musty odor
Water sitting more than 48–72 hours in warm conditions
Porous materials that have been wet for extended periods

removal_vs_drying.description: Don’t try to dry moldy materials in place in most cases. Source removal (cutting out affected material) is usually the cleaner, faster, and more defensible approach than attempting to dry and treat in place.

containment_and_controls.description: Engineering controls required when visible mold or Category 3 water is present.
requirements: Full containment with negative pressure is strongly recommended
Build a decon chamber (minimum 3x3, ideally 4x4) as an airlock when significant mold is present
Never move contaminated materials through clean zones without proper bagging
Use HEPA air scrubbing and negative air to control spores during disturbance
containment_and_controls.ppe: Upgrade PPE: respirator (N95 minimum, often P100 or higher), Tyvek or equivalent suit, gloves, and eye protection.

drying_approach_on_mold_jobs.description: Long-dwell or contaminated jobs often require slower, more controlled drying or wider removal to reduce the chance of hidden mold growth after drying is complete.

field_tips_and_working_rules.description: Practical rules of thumb used by experienced techs on mold and long-dwell water jobs.
items: Time is the enemy. Assume mold risk increases every day the materials stay wet, especially above 70°F and with poor airflow.
When in doubt on time or hidden moisture, treat it as higher risk. It’s better to contain and remove suspect materials than to dry and hope.
Category + Time + Warmth = Mold probability. Use this simple mental equation on every job.
Document conditions and timeline early. Photos of moisture, smell notes, and when the water started help later if mold becomes an issue.
Ventilation and airflow matter. Stagnant, warm, humid air is mold’s best friend. Moving air and controlling humidity slows growth even before you fully dry the materials.
Lack of musty odor does not guarantee low contamination. Mold and bacteria can be present and growing before a noticeable smell develops.

clean_water_stays_clean.description: Many techs assume that if water started clean, it remains low risk. In reality, clean water sitting for several days can develop significant bacterial and mold growth.

surface_clean_is_enough.description: Wiping surfaces or applying disinfectant does not address contamination that has already penetrated porous materials or grown behind finishes.

no_smell_means_no_contamination.description: Lack of musty odor does not guarantee low contamination. Mold and bacteria can be present and growing before a noticeable smell develops.

meta.description: Work in Progress Consolidated practical field knowledge for restoration equipment. Focuses on real-world usage, sizing, placement, monitoring, and common mistakes rather than manufacturer specs.
meta.scope: Water damage restoration equipment only. Product-specific specs and manuals are looked up on the fly.

general_principles: Equipment only works as well as the airflow and controls around it. Good placement and maintenance matter more than having the newest machines.
Always use GFCI protection on every piece of electrical equipment on water jobs. If not, do a load calculation and test before leaving.
Put a lipped mat or tray under dehumidifiers — leaks happen and secondary damage claims are expensive.
Check equipment performance daily. Just because it’s running doesn’t mean it’s working effectively.

types.lgr: Low Grain Refrigerant — most common modern unit. Can pull moisture to lower grains and works better in cooler conditions than older conventional units.
types.conventional: Older style refrigerant dehumidifiers. Less effective in cooler temperatures or when trying to reach very low humidity.
types.desiccant: Uses a desiccant wheel. Excellent in very cool conditions or when extremely low grains are needed. Less common on standard residential jobs.

sizing_rules_of_thumb.notes: These are simplified field guidelines. Always round up to the next whole unit. Size slightly high on day one — easier to turn units off later than to catch up.

placement: Place centrally to the affected area when possible. Consider ducting strategically. Direct towards wettest areas.
Ensure intake and exhaust have room to breathe (minimum 12–18 inches clearance).
Position so dry air exhaust helps push air toward wet areas or air mover intakes.
Route condensate drains with proper slope to a safe location (sink, toilet, outside, or condensate pump).
monitoring_performance: Check inlet vs outlet grains every morning. A healthy unit should typically pull 15–25 grains on an active job.
Watch relative humidity and GPP in the space. If numbers aren’t moving after several hours of proper operation, check airflow first.
Dehumidifiers work less efficiently at very high temperatures. Excessive heat can reduce moisture removal capacity on many LGR units.
common_mistakes: Poor placement (blocked intake/exhaust or bad airflow patterns).
Ignoring condensate drain issues (clogs, kinks, improper slope).
Leaving units running long after they’re no longer needed.

types.axial: High CFM, lower static pressure. Good for moving large volumes of air across open floors and surfaces. Most common type used on water jobs.
types.centrifugal_snail: Lower CFM but higher static pressure. Better at pushing air into cavities, under cabinets, or through wall drying systems.

placement_principles: Position fans to create directed airflow across wet surfaces rather than just circulating air in the room.
Aim for consistent coverage without dead zones. Create a 'wall of air' or gentle vortex — typically 45-degree angle to wet surfaces.
For floors: angle movers to lift moisture off the surface.
For wall cavities or baseboards: direct airflow into or along the cavity where possible.
Avoid short-circuiting (air mover intake pulling directly from another mover’s exhaust).
When drying bound moisture in wood, lower velocity airflow is often more effective than high-CFM blasting.
quantity_rules: Use 1 air mover per 10–14 linear feet of affected baseboard or wall as a starting point.
Add extra fans for wet ceilings, hallways, or rooms with many corners.
It’s usually better to have slightly too much airflow on day one than too little.
common_mistakes: Placing fans so they just blow air in circles without hitting wet surfaces.
Not getting airflow under LVP, carpet edges, or into cabinets.
Pointing fans at walls from too far away (air loses velocity before reaching the surface).
Forgetting to reposition fans as drying progresses.

portable_extractors.why_it_matters: Mechanical water removal is one of the highest-ROI activities. The more water you extract, the less work the air movers and dehumidifiers have to do.
technique: Work from the outer edges of the visible wet area inward toward the heaviest saturation.
When using a carpet wand near baseboards, set the wand down at the wall and pull outward — never push toward the baseboard.
Extract until you’re getting very little water on the last few passes. Rushing extraction usually means more work later.
when_especially_important: Class 2 and 3 water losses with carpet and pad.
Water under LVP or hardwood (when accessible).
Category 2 and 3 jobs (removing contaminated water reduces risk).
portable_extractors.limitations: Portable extractors can’t remove water that has already wicked deeply into materials or behind walls/ceilings. Use them aggressively where you can reach it, then shift to evaporation.

types.air_scrubbers: Designed to clean the air inside a space. Usually recirculate filtered air back into the room. Good for reducing airborne particles during and after work.
types.negative_air_machines: Designed to exhaust air out of the contained area. Create negative pressure when properly set up with containment.

when_to_use: Visible mold or suspected hidden mold
Jobs where you want to limit migration of spores/odors/particulates to unaffected areas
setup_tips: For negative air: Place the unit inside containment, preferably near the farthest point from the entry.
Exhaust filtered air outside the building when possible, away from intakes, windows, and doors.
Balance total CFM so negative pressure is maintained when all drying equipment is running.
Check and change filters regularly — dirty filters reduce effectiveness and can push contaminants through if damaged.
common_mistakes: Using air filtration as a substitute for proper containment and source removal.
Poor exhaust setup (exhausting contaminated air into clean areas).
Not changing filters often enough on dirty jobs.

ulv_foggers.purpose: Powered high volume spray bottle, essentially. Applies liquid antimicrobial, disinfectant, and odor control products as a fine mist (typically 5–50 micron droplets). Used to treat surfaces and air spaces that are difficult to reach with conventional spraying.
when_to_use: After gross water removal on Category 2 or 3 losses
During mold remediation on non-porous and semi-porous surfaces inside containment
For odor neutralization in affected areas
Treating wall cavities, under cabinets, crawl spaces, or other confined/hard-to-reach areas
when_not_to_use: As a shortcut instead of proper demo, extraction, and drying
On surfaces that will be disturbed or removed before required dwell time
Without following the specific product’s EPA label and SDS exactly
Near sensitive electronics, artwork, or materials that cannot tolerate added moisture
safety_requirements: Full PPE is mandatory (respirator, gloves, eye protection, Tyvek or equivalent suit)
Evacuate all people and pets from the treatment area
Post clear 'Do Not Enter' signage with product-specific re-entry time
Turn off and isolate the HVAC system
Never fog near open flames, sparks, or energized electrical equipment
operation_notes: Calculate cubic volume and amount of diluted product required.
Mix exactly according to label instructions — never guess on dilution.
Start at the point farthest from the exit and work systematically toward the door.
Keep the fogger moving in slow, slightly overlapping passes.
Allow full labeled dwell/contact time before re-entry.

quick_assessment_order: Approximate square footage of affected area
What materials are wet and how high
Are there any small rooms or closed doors?
sizing_and_capacity.small_spaces_rule: Any room with a door that will remain closed (bathrooms, closets, laundry rooms, etc.) should usually get its own dedicated dehumidifier, regardless of square footage.
sizing_and_capacity.combined_air_volumes: When you remove an interior wall, two separate air volumes become one larger air volume. You do not always need to treat them separately if one properly sized dehumidifier can handle the combined volume.

placement_and_usage.core_idea: Air should move across wet materials, not just circulate in the room. Dehumidifiers remove moisture from the air — they only work well when air movers are delivering moist air to them.
key_tips: Generally move air across wet surfaces rather than directly into them. Create consistent circular airflow throughout the affected area.
Prevent overheating in small spaces — always use a humidistat and monitor temperature daily.
Match your drying approach to the loss type: Fast, clean leaks can usually be dried more aggressively. Slow leaks with deep penetration should use a more gradual approach.
Place dehumidifiers to pull from the wettest or most contaminated zone when possible, or centrally for even coverage.

taping_and_routing.description: Proper securing of power cords and condensate drain lines to prevent trip hazards, equipment damage, and premature pump failure.
required: All power cords and hoses that cross walkways or traffic areas must be taped down flat.
Use only blue painter's tape on finished floors. Never use duct tape or gaffer's tape.
Keep condensate drain lines as straight as possible with minimal dips or loops to prevent standing water in the hose.
Secure drain lines with blue tape when running up walls or countertops to sinks, showers, or tubs.
Tape cords and hoses every few feet so they cannot shift or create trip hazards.
taping_and_routing.voice_prompt: Tape down every cord and hose that crosses a walkway. Use blue tape only on finished floors. Keep drain lines as straight as possible — avoid big loops and low spots.

daily_checks: Start at the dehumidifier and record inlet vs outlet RH and grains
Check that every air mover is running and properly aimed
Take moisture readings in the exact same locations as Day 1
Check for cold spots and musty odors
Make at least one intentional adjustment based on observations
performance_monitoring.temperature_and_humidity: Temperature and humidity are equally important. Focus on achieving low grains. Keep the space between 75–85°F while ensuring dehumidifiers are actively removing moisture.

job_functions.description: Restoration rarely follows a perfectly linear path. You will often move back and forth between phases (especially extraction ↔ selective_demolition ↔ drying_and_monitoring). The first phase and last phase are almost always required. Use these phases as a mental map and decision framework, not a rigid checklist.

service_contract.description: Obtain signed authorization from the material interested party before any physical work begins. This is a non-negotiable legal and billing requirement.
key_activities: Present scope of work and obtain signature on work authorization and any required releases
Confirm the signer has authority to approve work
Document any limitations or special instructions at time of signing
critical_decision_points: Has the correct person signed?
Are there scope limitations that need to be noted before starting?
practical_guidance: Never begin physical work (including shutting off water or moving equipment) until the authorization is signed — this protects everyone.
Many homeowners are overwhelmed; keep the explanation short and professional: 'I need your signature before I can start any work. This protects both of us.'
If the homeowner is hesitant, calmly explain that no work can begin (and no billing can occur) without it.
Document any verbal instructions or concerns the customer gives you at signing time.
service_contract.voice_considerations: Keep this phase short and firm. Do not negotiate or start working while they 'look for the paperwork.'

initial_assessment.description: First on-site evaluation. Identify hazards, determine category and class, verify the source, set realistic Day 1 scope, and decide required engineering controls.
key_activities: Initial moisture readings on exposed surfaces
critical_decision_points: What is the water category? (Err high if unsure)
Are engineering controls required before intrusive work?
Is the Day 1 scope realistic?
practical_guidance: Day 1 is almost never full demo + complete drying. Focus on signed paperwork, source control, bulk extraction, basic containment, and drying setup.
Visible water is often not the origin — hunt for hidden COL behind walls, under floors, or from internal sources.
Internal plumbing failures (toilet supply lines, fridge ice makers, washing machine hoses) are more common than most people assume.
On Category 2 or 3, apply disinfectant and surface wipe before doing a full moisture survey or moving extensively through the space.
If a ceiling is sagging, stabilize it with a zip pole as soon as the water is shut off — ideally before or right after source control.
Initial moisture readings are only on whatever surface is currently exposed. You cannot reliably read the subfloor until surface materials are removed or lifted.
initial_assessment.voice_considerations: Walk the tech through this phase step-by-step. Strongly emphasize 'Do not touch anything until authorization is signed' and the Category 3 / visible mold control gate.

source_control.description: Stop the water at its origin and verify it has truly stopped before proceeding.
key_activities: Locate and shut off water at angle stop or main
Verify the water has actually stopped (visual confirmation)
critical_decision_points: Is the water truly stopped?
Do I need a plumber or utility involvement?
practical_guidance: Never assume the valve fully stopped the flow. For toilet supply line failures, open the tank and hold the flapper open to confirm no more water is entering.
Visual confirmation at the source is required before moving to extraction.
If you cannot stop the water, escalate immediately — do not proceed with extraction while it is still actively leaking.
source_control.voice_considerations: 'After shutting the water off, I need to verify it's actually stopped. For a toilet supply line, that usually means opening the tank and holding the flapper.'

extraction.description: Mechanical removal of standing and extractable liquid water to reduce drying load and secondary damage risk.
critical_decision_points: Are controls verified before disturbing contaminated water?
Which materials must be removed now vs dried in place?
practical_guidance: Work extraction from the outer edges of the visible wet area inward toward the heaviest saturation. Starting in the center pushes moisture further into unaffected areas.
When using a carpet extraction wand near baseboards, set the wand down at the wall and pull outward — never push toward the baseboard. The lip will drive water under the baseboard if you push.
Most experienced techs extract first, then build full containment. Full plastic before extraction is usually the wrong order on wet jobs because it’s slower and messier.
Put a lipped mat or tray under dehumidifiers in case they leak — it’s cheap insurance against secondary damage claims.
Use GFCI protection on every piece of electrical equipment. If not, do a load calculation and test before leaving for the day.
extraction.voice_considerations: Emphasize technique details: outer-to-inner extraction and the carpet wand baseboard rule. These small habits dramatically improve results.

contents_handling.description: Assessment, protection, cleaning, pack-out, pack-in/around (on property storage), or disposal of personal property affected by the loss.
key_activities: Proper packing, labeling, and chain of custody
critical_decision_points: Clean and store on site vs clean and pack-out?
Which items are high-value or sentimental?
practical_guidance: Photograph everything before you touch it — wide shots + detail shots. This is non-negotiable on anything valuable or disputed.
Sort on-site first: create three piles (Trash, Pack-out, Clean-on-site). This saves time and money downstream.
Work from least damaged to most damaged to avoid re-contaminating clean items.
Be aggressive with trash on heavily contaminated porous items (fabric, particle board, insulation) after long dwell times — it is often cheaper and faster than trying to clean them.
Use clean staging areas with poly or ram board. Never set dirty or sooty items directly on clean surfaces.
Don't handle drugs, money, or weapons.
contents_handling.voice_considerations: Contents work is where you either build trust or destroy it. Homeowners remember how you handled their stuff more than how straight the drywall is.

engineering_controls.description: Establishing containment, negative pressure, air filtration, and other controls to manage contamination and airflow.
key_activities: Install floor protection and critical barriers early
Set up and verify negative pressure / HEPA scrubbing
If mold is more than a few square feet, consider a decon chamber or external door to working area access.
critical_decision_points: What level of containment is actually required?
Is negative pressure holding under load?
practical_guidance: Use a framing laser for fast, straight tape lines when hanging poly — it dramatically improves speed and quality.
When pulling plastic tigh wall to wall into double sided tape, place the edge about a finger tip width beyond before sticking, which gives just enough flex not to over stress the tape.
Place the negative air machine on the wall furthest from the door. Install makeup air on the same wall as the door but as far from the machine as possible.
Keep containment 18 inches to 2 feet beyond affected materials when possible.

On very small bathrooms you usually don’t need negative pressure — you can place equipment in the bathtub or on a vanity to save floor space. But of there is a window, take advantage of the neg pressure because it's a smaller space and you can kick dust into the house easily.
Re-verify containment integrity and negative pressure at the start of every monitoring visit, especially on Category 2/3 or mold jobs.
engineering_controls.voice_considerations: Practical sequence most techs actually use: quick floor protection + extraction first, then proper containment. Full walls before extraction is often inefficient on residential jobs.

containment.coreMindset: Always take the opportunity to build plastic. It’s a billable item that’s cheap for the customer, saves equipment runtime, and shows you’re being proactive and professional on the job. Reducing air volume is one of the highest-ROI moves you can make.

whenToUse.always: On every job, regardless of category — even clean Category 1
whenToUse.airVolume: Anytime you can reduce air volume to make equipment more effective
whenToUse.demo: Before any demolition or dust-creating work
whenToUse.isolation: To isolate the affected area instead of conditioning the whole house

recommendedSequence: Lay floor protection immediately upon arrival
Build plastic to isolate the affected area
Set up full containment and negative pressure if required for contamination control
Marking layout first: After deciding where you want containment, use double-sided tape to mark out exactly where the plastic is going. This way everything is ready once you cut your plastic.

Tape application technique: Hold the roll in one hand and pull out a long strip with the other. Stick just the end on the edge where you want to straddle, then pull and lay the whole strip down at once. Smooth it over, then pull out the next long section. Scoot across the floor on your knees — it’s fast once you get the rhythm. This technique works for both blue tape and double-sided tape.
Double-sided tape ripping technique: While ripping double-sided tape, give it a quick jerking back-and-forth motion. This makes the outer release liner much easier to peel off later.
Cutting plastic: Cut the plastic off the roll first before hanging. Consider using scissors instead of a knife when working near expensive finished surfaces.
Plastic orientation tip: The clean factory-cut edge of the 10-foot side can be placed right along the edge of the double-sided tape as you hang it.
Extra material: Give yourself an extra foot or more where the plastic meets the wall. Having a little extra is much cheaper and better than coming up short and having to patch it.

Practical hanging method: Use 10-foot wide plastic, oriented vertically. Pull out the length you need, get on a ladder, attach it across the ceiling to double-sided tape first, then work your way down the sides and bottom.

Side attachment technique: When attaching plastic to the sides, work back and forth — go a few feet down the left side, then switch to the right side, then back to the left, alternating sides as you work your way down. This keeps the plastic tight and even.
On particularly dirty Category 2 and definitely on Category 3 jobs, apply a second layer of blue tape along the seam where the plastic meets the double-sided tape on the wall.
Use ZipWall poles to support the plastic — especially useful on popcorn ceilings or when the plastic is heavy. Start your first corner with a ZipWall pole if needed.
Running negative pressure? You’ll need more poles. The smaller the room, the more poles per foot you need to keep the plastic tight and prevent it from sucking in.
When pulling plastic wall-to-wall into double-sided tape, leave about a fingertip width of extra material before sticking it — this gives just enough flex so you don’t overstress the tape.

Negative air return filter installation: Use the same size filter as your negative air machine. Tape it securely from the outside of the containment with airflow pointing inward. Then go inside and cut the plastic on the right, left, and bottom edges to create an inward-pulling flap.

Negative air transition: Once contaminants have been removed and cleaning is complete, switch the negative air machine to static/recirculation mode inside the room. This helps retain the warm, dry air inside the space.

zipperDoors.singleZipper: Place the zipper so the bottom sits right where the plastic meets the floor. Cut two 45-degree angles about 18 inches wide at the bottom (starting roughly 4 inches outside each side of the zipper) to create a flap with no trip hazard. Run the zipper up 2–4 feet, then add another set of 45-degree angles near the top, leaving 1–2 feet at the ceiling. This creates a wide C-shaped opening that’s easy to scoot equipment through.
zipperDoors.doubleZipper: Install two zippers 3–4 feet apart running straight up and down. Cut straight across the bottom between them. This creates a full-width door that can be rolled up, which is much better when moving furniture, dealing with accessibility, or frequently moving equipment in and out.
zipperDoors.placementNote: Place doorways in locations that are convenient for both workflow and the customer, if they need to move through the area.

types: Full containment with negative pressure and airlock

selective_demolition.description: Controlled removal of materials that cannot be reliably dried or that present unacceptable risk if left in place. Selective demolition should be strategic and minimal — only remove what is necessary to allow proper drying or eliminate unacceptable risk.
key_activities: Assess before cutting — check for asbestos, lead, hidden utilities, and structural concerns
Determine remove vs dry-in-place decisions using material type, category, and dwell time
Work top-down and outside-in when possible
Use surgical precision around electrical, plumbing, HVAC, and customer belongings
Protect all surfaces and contents that will remain
Set up dust and debris containment during demo
Proper bagging and removal of contaminated materials
Documentation of what was removed and justification
critical_decision_points: Remove vs dry in place? (Consider material type, category, dwell time, and drying feasibility)
How aggressive should removal be? (Category + dwell time are major drivers)
Can we use targeted methods (drill holes, flood cuts, access points) instead of full removal?
Do we need lead/asbestos testing before starting major demo?

generally_remove: Wet insulation (fiberglass, cellulose) on most jobs
Particle board / MDF that is significantly swollen or disintegrating
Drywall on Category 3 or long-dwell jobs
Carpet pad that is saturated (especially Cat 2/3 or >24 hrs)
Materials with visible mold growth or strong musty odor
Heavily contaminated porous materials with extended dwell time
consider_minimal_removal_first: Dimensional lumber / framing (drill holes or targeted access when possible)
Cabinets (drill holes under toe kicks and into wall cavity behind instead of full removal when feasible)
Category 1 drywall with short dwell and no physical compromise

practical_guidance: Use a knife on painted and caulked edges when removing finished materials like casing and backsplash to avoid ripping the paint.
Assess before you cut. Never start cutting without first checking for asbestos, lead, hidden utilities, and structural issues.
Work from the top down: fixtures and cabinets first, then baseboards, then walls, then flooring. This reduces re-contamination risk and improves debris control.
Protect what stays. Use Ram Board, heavy builder’s paper, or plywood on finished surfaces. Secure with low-adhesive blue painter’s tape only.
On Category 3 losses with long dwell time, cut higher than visible damage — typically 12–18 inches minimum on walls, going to the next logical two-foot interval when needed.
Use surgical precision around electrical, plumbing, and HVAC. Remove outlet covers before cutting and work carefully around wiring.
When possible, use targeted access methods instead of full removal. Drill holes under cabinets and into wall cavities behind them to inject airflow rather than removing entire sections.
If removing sections of cabinets, install temporary shoring under countertops to maintain structural support.
Set up dust and debris containment (plastic, zip poles, or barriers) before starting demo to protect unaffected areas.
Cap opened water supply lines with pressure caps even if the valve is off — it reduces liability and looks professional.
Document what was removed and the reasoning. Photos and notes protect everyone if scope questions arise later.

category_specific_guidance.category_1: More conservative. Prioritize minimal removal and targeted drying methods when possible.
category_specific_guidance.category_2: Moderate approach. Higher chance of needing to remove saturated porous materials, especially with any significant dwell time.
category_specific_guidance.category_3: More aggressive by default. Extended dwell time significantly increases hidden microbial risk behind finishes. Cut higher than visible damage and remove more porous materials.

safety_and_hazmat: Test for lead and asbestos before major demo on homes built before 1980 (or when suspect materials are present). Baseboards and outlet covers can often be removed safely if drywall remains intact.
On lath and plaster ceilings/walls (common in older homes), expect chicken wire, mixed fasteners, and hidden cavities. A carbide multi-tool is often more effective than a reciprocating saw.
Wood paneling in basements is frequently installed over furring strips, creating hidden cavities that require attention on Category 3 jobs.
Remove exposed fasteners, nails, and screws as you work. Leaving hazards behind creates unnecessary risk.
common_mistakes: Starting demo without proper pre-assessment (missing asbestos, lead, or utilities)
Over-cutting and removing more material than necessary
Under-cutting on Category 3 or long-dwell jobs (leaving contaminated material that later causes callbacks)
Failing to protect finished surfaces and contents that will remain
Not setting up dust containment before starting demo
Not documenting what was removed and why
Working bottom-up or inside-out, which increases cross-contamination
selective_demolition.voice_considerations: Emphasize strategic, minimal demolition rather than aggressive removal. Over-demoing increases cost and time. Under-demoing on contaminated jobs creates bigger problems later. Focus on 'assess before you cut' and protecting what stays.

demo_order.name: Demo Order – Top Down, Outside In
demo_order.description: Standard physical removal sequence for any room. consider working top-down and outside-in.
steps: 1. Remove all trim — door casing, window trim, baseboards, quarter round, and cove molding. Pull all protruding nails.
2. Remove outlet and switch covers, light fixtures, and any wall-mounted heaters.
3. Remove all wall-mounted fixtures and accessories — mirrors, towel bars, toilet paper holders, soap dishes, grab bars, etc.

4. Sink & Toilet Decision: Shut off water supplies. For sinks: disconnect water lines and drain, then install compression caps on the lines. For toilets: shut off water, flush to empty the tank, disconnect supply line, and plug the drain with a drain plug.

5. Cabinet & Vanity Decision: Remove upper cabinets, lower cabinets, vanities, full-height cabinets, and countertops if affected material is behind or underneath them. Save undamaged cabinets and countertops for reinstallation.
7. Appliance Decision: Disconnect electrical power and water lines as needed, then pull appliances — refrigerator, range, dishwasher, washing machine, dryer, water heater.
8. Tile & Tub Decision: Remove tile walls, tub surrounds, bathtubs, showers, or toilets if affected material is behind or underneath.
9. Cut and remove wall and ceiling material — drywall, plaster, or tile wall — then pull all nails and screws from studs and ceiling joists.
11. Flooring Decision: Remove flooring (carpet, pad, tack strip, laminate, vinyl, hardwood, tile, underlayment, or glue-down carpet) when appropriate.

demo_final_cleaning.description: Foundational cleaning and sanitization after demolition (and as needed during the job) to remove dust, debris, and contaminants before drying begins. This step is critical for air quality, preventing callbacks, and preparing surfaces for drying equipment. It may seem tedious, to think about it as procedural steps. Instead, think of it more like baking than rocket science, and know you can adjust accordingly. These steps are designed to minimize strain, effort and repetition while maximizing product quality. A suggestion, not a rule.
key_activities: Blow medium particulates down top to bottom (blower, ulv fogger liquid off, or a fan in a bind, then vac floor again. This helps remove dirt from corners vacs cannot reach. Outlets etc.
Wipe down all exposed framing, plumbing, wires, and electrical boxes, horizontal surfaces.
Final detailed vacuuming (especially corners, base plates, and gaps)
ULV fogging with appropriate disinfectant/sanitizer when required.
Application of stain remover/sealer on exposed wood framing (when justified)
Ongoing cleaning during drying and monitoring visits as needed

alt.title: Pressure Wash + Extractor Method for Contaminated Sill Plates
conditions: Concrete slab floor only — never use on wood subfloors
Heavy contamination present (sewer backup with sludge or groundwater flood carrying dirt/silt)
3. Apply liberal disinfectant along the base plate and sill plate, let it dwell
4. Pressure wash framing and slab while running a portable extractor the entire time
5. After, Run dehumidifier and allow the structure to dry completely
7. ULV air blast and dust settle
