9. Wipe down all framing, plumbing, wires, and electrical boxes
The air blast → vacuum → wipe order ensures dust is knocked down and removed before wiping surfaces.
Never pressure wash if there is a wood subfloor — it drives contamination deeper into the wood.

critical_decision_points: Is this a standard post-demo clean or a heavier Category 3 / mold decontamination?
Should ULV fogging be used, will it save me time or muscle, and with what product?
Has cleaning inside electrical boxes and hard-to-reach areas been completed?
practical_guidance: After demo, follow this foundational sequence: 1) Remove all heavy debris, 2) Vacuum the floor, 3) ULV air blast only to knock dust out of framing and wires, 4) Vacuum the floor again, 5) Wipe down all framing, plumbing, wires, and electrical boxes, 6) Final floor vacuum paying special attention to corners along the base plate and any gaps, 7) ULV fog with disinfectant/sanitizer.
Dust collects behind outlets and switches where the vacuum often can’t reach — make a point to clean inside electrical boxes during the wipe-down step.
When fogging with ULV, apply enough product to get a nice even coat with slight surface wetting. The wood should absorb some but should not be dripping wet.
Use microfiber rags for wiping. Fold the rag in half twice to create 8 clean surfaces per rag. Save and wash the rags — they are reusable.

On Category 3 jobs with heavy contamination on concrete slab floors and framing sitting directly on concrete, consider the pressure wash + portable extractor method while running the extractor the entire time (never use this on wood subfloors).
ULV fogging is a supplemental treatment tool, not a replacement for proper extraction, cleaning, and drying. Only use products where the EPA label specifically allows fogging.
After disinfectant has dried on mold remediation jobs, apply stain remover (formerly branded as MMR) to all exposed wood framing for aesthetic purposes.
Choose disinfectant type based on category: botanical for Category 2, accelerated oxidizer for Category 3 when fogging.
demo_final_cleaning.voice_considerations: This phase is often rushed or skipped in the field. Emphasize that a clean, dust-free structure dries better and has fewer callbacks. The post-demo cleaning sequence should be treated as a repeatable checklist, not optional.
demo_final_cleaning.real_world_notes: Many techs under-estimate how much dust and debris remains after demo. Thorough base cleaning dramatically improves air quality and reduces the chance of musty odors developing later during drying.

meta.description: Practical field knowledge for plumbing and electrical considerations on water damage and restoration jobs. Focuses on safety, common issues, when to escalate, and real-world habits.

common_locations: Near the water meter (often outside or in basement/crawlspace)
Inside the house near where the main line enters (utility room, garage, basement)
Under kitchen or bathroom sinks (localized shutoffs)
field_tips: Always try to locate the main shutoff early, even if the leak appears localized.
On older houses, shutoffs can be seized, missing handles, or in awkward locations. Don’t assume they’ll work smoothly.
After shutting off the water, always verify it has actually stopped (visual confirmation).

common_issues: Supply line failures (toilet supply lines, fridge ice maker lines, washing machine hoses) are very common failure points.
Drain line issues — clogs, collapsed lines, or failed joints can cause slow leaks or backups.
Appliance-related failures (washing machines, dishwashers, water heaters, ice makers).
Fixture failures (toilet tank components, faucet cartridges, shower valves).
when_to_involve_a_licensed_plumber: The source of the leak is not obvious or accessible.
There is significant damage to plumbing lines (not just a supply hose).
The job involves modifying or rerouting plumbing.
There are signs of major issues (low pressure, multiple leaks, sewage involvement).
The homeowner or adjuster wants a professional plumbing assessment.

old_plumbing_considerations.galvanized_steel: Common in older homes. Prone to corrosion and restricted flow. Can hide leaks inside walls or under floors.
old_plumbing_considerations.polybutylene_pb: Used in some homes from the late 1970s to mid-1990s. Known for failures at fittings. Flag it if seen.
old_plumbing_considerations.copper: Generally reliable but can develop pinhole leaks in certain water conditions.
old_plumbing_considerations.pex: Common in newer construction. Generally reliable but can be damaged by improper installation or physical impact.

field_tips: Internal plumbing failures are more common than external sources on many residential jobs.
Put pressure caps on opened water supply lines even if the valve is secure. It looks professional and avoids liability.
Be careful not to create new leaks while moving or working around fixtures and appliances.
On higher contamination jobs, be extra careful about how water is being moved to avoid backflow into clean water supplies.

gfci_protection.rule: Non-negotiable on any job involving water (or even potential water). All equipment must be protected by GFCI.
field_tips: Use GFCI pigtails or plug equipment into GFCI-protected outlets.
Never bypass or ignore GFCI requirements — even on 'clean' water jobs.
If it plugs in and there’s water involved, it needs GFCI protection.

common_hazards: Standing water near outlets, switches, light fixtures, or appliances is extremely dangerous.
Water can travel through walls and reach electrical components you can’t see.
Burned or damaged electrical panels, outlets, or wiring are common on fire jobs and older water losses.
Hidden corrosion on connections can develop over time after water exposure.
when_to_involve_a_licensed_electrician: There is standing water near electrical panels, outlets, or switches.
Electrical components have been burned or heavily smoked.
You need to verify power is off to an area.
There is damaged or exposed wiring.
The job requires moving, modifying, or repairing electrical components.
Aluminum wiring is present and work is needed in those areas.

old_electrical_systems.aluminum_wiring: Used in many homes built between ~1965–1975. Requires special connectors and handling. Can be a fire hazard if not addressed properly. Note it and be cautious about disturbing connections.
old_electrical_systems.knob_and_tube: Found in very old homes. Fragile and often not up to modern code. Water exposure makes it even more hazardous.

field_tips: Never assume power is off. Verify it.
Use a non-contact voltage tester when appropriate.
Keep extension cords and equipment out of standing water.
Treat any electrical component that has been exposed to water or fire as potentially compromised until verified safe.
Slow down around electrical systems. Rushing electrical decisions is how people get hurt.
electrical.lockout_tagout: On some jobs (especially commercial), proper lockout/tagout procedures should be followed. Even on residential jobs, clearly communicating that power is off to an area helps prevent accidents.

combined_considerations: Water + electricity is one of the fastest ways to get seriously injured or killed. Always treat it with extreme caution.
On older homes, both plumbing and electrical systems are often outdated and more hazardous when water is involved.
If you're unsure about the safety of the plumbing or electrical system, or if the work goes beyond basic equipment setup, escalate to a licensed professional early.
Document any plumbing or electrical concerns you observe. This protects everyone if issues arise later.

atmospheric_testing.required: Test atmosphere anytime gas lines, propane, or sewer gas odors are present.
atmospheric_testing.recommended: Carry a 4-gas monitor on every crawlspace job.
atmospheric_testing.voiceNote: If there’s a gas line down here, I’m testing. No gas line, no need.

respirator.approach: Use professional judgment based on conditions.
respirator.recommended: Wear a respirator with a properly rated filter anytime there’s visible dust, disturbed insulation, Category 2/3 water, or musty odors.
respirator.situational: On a tall, clean, well-maintained Category 1 crawlspace with a fresh vapor barrier, many techs will skip the respirator for quick entries.
respirator.voiceNote: If it’s dusty or dirty at all, I’m wearing a respirator. If it’s clean and I’m just running in for a minute, I might skip it.

other: Knee pads are highly recommended if you want to still be doing this job in ten years.
Headlamp with backup batteries is strongly recommended.
Gloves and coveralls are situational based on how dirty the crawlspace is.

rules: Never work alone in a crawlspace
Always have a spotter at the access point
Lay down plywood or Ram Board from the hatch to your work area
Stay on floor joists whenever possible — avoid walking on insulation or vapor barrier
Hand materials out — don’t throw them
Leave the crawlspace cleaner than you found it

manpower.principle: Many hands makes light work — especially in crawlspaces.
manpower.voiceNote: If you’ve got four guys on site and the crawlspace can use the help, get three of them down there. Knock it out fast.

key_things_to_look_for: Water staining or discoloration on the underside of floor joists, subfloor, and sill plates
Damaged or collapsed storage (cardboard boxes, paper products, etc.)
Sagging or improperly supported HVAC ductwork
Disturbed or broken plumbing straps and hangers
Corrosion or dissolved sections on the underside of pipes (especially from drain cleaners like Drano)
Standing water or ponding on the ground
Condition of the existing vapor barrier (rips, pooling, mold underneath)
inspection.voiceNote: When I drop into a crawlspace, I’m looking for staining on the joists, sagging insulation, messed up HVAC, broken plumbing straps, and pipe damage — especially on the bottom of pipes from stuff like Drano.

wet_insulation.core_rule: Get wet insulation off the wood framing as fast as possible.
wet_insulation.best_practice: Remove all wet insulation from the affected area the same day it is dropped.
wet_insulation.field_reality: If it’s a large area, drop all the wet insulation to the ground immediately, then remove it as soon as practical — even if that means coming back the next day.
wet_insulation.voiceNote: If there’s wet insulation touching the joists, I want it all on the ground today. Better to drop it all and come back tomorrow than leave any of it hanging on the wood.

air_movement_and_pressure.core_rule: Avoid pushing air from the crawlspace up into the living space.
key_guidance: When ducting a dehumidifier into a crawlspace, you are creating positive pressure. Make sure the air has a clear path out through foundation vents rather than back up through the hatch or subfloor.
Never push air into the crawlspace through a vent — this creates positive pressure and can drive contaminants into the house.
Two good solutions: Place a fan inside the crawlspace and duct it out through a vent, or build containment over the access point with an AFD running inside the contained area.
air_movement_and_pressure.voiceNote: Don’t push crawlspace air up into the house. If I’m piping a dehu in here, I make sure that air gets out through the vents, not back up the hatch.

localized_tenting.description: Creating a targeted drying chamber under a specific wet area.
method: Cut a piece of plastic the height of the crawlspace and skirt it around the affected area to create a smaller chamber.
Duct dehumidifier output from outside through the hatch directly into this localized chamber.
Use clear plastic when possible to allow light in.
Seal off nearby crawlspace vents in that zone from the outside if needed.
Particularly effective for drying bound moisture in subfloor from below on slow leak jobs.
localized_tenting.voiceNote: Got a slow leak that soaked the subfloor? I’m building a little tent under it in the crawlspace and pumping dry air straight to it.

negative_air_setup.description: Optional improvisation for creating airflow/negative pressure.
negative_air_setup.method: Place a regular axial fan with layflat ducting strategically in the crawlspace and run the duct out through a foundation vent. This pulls fresh air in through the hatch and other vents.
negative_air_setup.voiceNote: One trick I like is dropping a fan with layflat ducting and running it out a side vent. Pulls good airflow through the whole space.

ulv_fogging.recommendation: Many experienced techs consider the ULV fogger the best tool for cleaning crawlspaces.
advantages: Reaches into all the small gaps and framing spaces effectively
Knocks dust and debris off the underside of joists and subfloor
Much faster and easier than using a pump sprayer or spray bottle
ulv_fogging.practical_use: Run an extension cord into the crawlspace, fog the area thoroughly, then come back through with rags and a wire brush to wipe down the framing.
ulv_fogging.voiceNote: In a crawlspace, I’d rather use the ULV fogger than a pump sprayer any day. Fog it down, then come back through with rags and a wire brush.

vapor_barrier.guidance: Repair damaged sections during the job when practical. Replacing the vapor barrier after drying is often a billable upgrade on long-term wet jobs.

code_requirements.legal_note: Proper vapor barrier installation is governed by building code and is legally required.
key_requirements: Joints must overlap by a minimum of 6 inches and be sealed or taped
Material must extend at least 6 inches up the stem wall
It must be attached and sealed to the foundation wall
Terminate at the top of the masonry — do not run it up onto the wood sill plate
code_requirements.voiceNote: When we replace a vapor barrier, it’s not just a recommendation — it’s code.

ultra_ppe.when_to_use: For crawlspaces with heavy sewage contamination, large insect infestations, extreme filth, or heavy mold.
ultra_ppe.setup: White Tyvek suit → latex gloves taped at wrist → yellow suit over the top → demolition gloves taped over the latex gloves.
ultra_ppe.voiceNote: If this crawlspace is straight nasty — sewage, bugs everywhere — I’m running the double suit setup.

drying_and_monitoring.description: Active drying using equipment combined with ongoing data collection to verify progress toward drying goals.
key_activities: Daily monitoring walks (same locations, consistent data)

meta.description: Knowledge base for local tenting as a targeted drying technique. Used to concentrate drying power on specific problem areas that are difficult to dry with open-room methods.

tenting.description: Local tenting involves creating temporary enclosures using poly sheeting to isolate and dry specific wet areas more effectively. It allows you to pipe dry air directly to the problem area while controlling temperature and humidity within the tent.
when_to_use: Bound moisture in subfloors (especially under vaulted or high ceilings where open-room drying is inefficient)
Behind or under cabinets and toe kicks
Underside of finished floors you want to save
Small, confined wet areas where full-room drying would be wasteful or ineffective
Targeted drying of wall cavities or specific structural assemblies
when_not_to_use: Large open areas (better served by standard equipment placement)
Low-evaporation assemblies like tile unless water intrusion is minimal
Situations where creating and maintaining the tent would cause more damage than it solves

basic_requirements: Use 6-mil or thicker fire-retardant poly sheeting
Seal the tent as well as practical to contain dry air
Pipe a dehumidifier directly into the tent (positive pressure into the enclosure)
Include an air vent or exhaust path unless the tent opens into a crawlspace
Use a humidistat inside the tent to control conditions
Keep temperatures reasonable — avoid excessive heat inside the tent
best_practices: Make the tent as small as possible while still covering the wet area effectively
Monitor temperature and humidity inside the tent regularly
Ensure good airflow across the wet surfaces inside the tent (small air movers or directed ducting can help)
Check for condensation on the inside of the poly — adjust airflow or temperature if it becomes excessive
Document the tent setup with photos for the job file

field_tips: Local tenting works especially well when you have bound moisture in subfloors under vaulted ceilings — it concentrates the drying effort instead of trying to condition the entire large air volume.
Under cabinets and toe kicks: Use existing gaps or carefully lift the toe kick to create access, then tent the area.
Under bathtubs: Tenting can be very effective if you can get dry air underneath without major demolition.
Always use a humidistat inside the tent. Without one, you risk over-drying or creating condensation issues.
If the tent is venting into a crawlspace, you usually don’t need an additional vent. If venting into the living space, include a controlled exhaust path.
Local tenting is often more effective than aggressive high-velocity air movers alone on materials with bound moisture.
Combine with low-velocity airflow inside the tent when dealing with wood or other sensitive materials.
limitations_and_risks: Creates additional work to build and later remove the tent
Can trap moisture if not properly vented or monitored
Not effective on low-permeability materials like tile unless very small water volume
Risk of condensation on the poly if temperature differential is too great
Requires monitoring — you can’t just set it and forget it
related_techniques: Wall cavity drying (flood cuts + directed air or negative pressure)
Targeted equipment placement without full tenting (sometimes sufficient)

critical_decision_points: Are we on track with drying goals?
Is moisture rising or new issues appearing?
practical_guidance: Walk every drying job the exact same way every day. Start at the dehumidifier, record inlet vs outlet grains, check every air mover, take moisture readings in the same locations as Day 1, and make at least one intentional adjustment.
Check dehumidifier performance every morning — a healthy unit should typically pull 15–25 grains on an active job.
Pay special attention to commonly missed wet areas: under and behind cabinets, subfloor underneath base plates, California corners, inside vanity kickboards, and under shower pans.
When drying bound moisture in wood, lower velocity airflow is often more effective than high-CFM blasting. It allows better heat transfer and creates a more gradual moisture gradient.
Small rooms can overheat quickly. Always use a humidistat in small spaces and monitor temperature daily. Never leave a small contained space without temperature control.
If the job isn’t drying well after two days, look first at airflow across wet surfaces, then at hidden moisture sources, then at equipment sizing.
drying_and_monitoring.voice_considerations: This is one of the highest-value phases for daily support. Structure monitoring as a repeatable checklist the tech can follow verbally.

general_cleaning.It could be ANYTHING: This is considered self evident and infinate in variety to the point that there is no right way. Think about protecting yourself, material and bodily cost, proper chemicals and applications, and bodily efficiency in time and wear. This section can be expanded later.

final_verification.description: Final checks to confirm materials have reached acceptable moisture levels and no hidden issues remain before customer sign-off.
key_activities: Final moisture verification at established points
Controlled teardown only after verification passes
critical_decision_points: Have all materials met drying goals with data?
Are there remaining borderline readings or hidden concerns?
practical_guidance: Never begin teardown of containment and equipment until final moisture verification has passed. Tearing down early is one of the fastest ways to create callbacks.
Check previously elevated or suspect areas one more time, including wall cavities, subfloors, and insulation zones.
Walk the customer through every room that was affected. Show before/after photos and explain what was done in plain language.
Document any remaining repair needs clearly and provide written recommendations.
final_verification.voice_considerations: Be deliberate. This phase is about defensibility as much as technical completion. 'We don’t tear down until the data says we’re done.'

closeout_and_documentation.description: Equipment removal, customer communication, warranty discussion, and complete job documentation.
key_activities: Review of work performed and remaining items
critical_decision_points: Does the customer understand what was done and what to watch for?
practical_guidance: Over-document rather than under-document. Good photos and logs save more headaches than they create.
Photograph pre-existing damage thoroughly before starting work and note any homeowner statements about prior damage.
Clearly separate new damage from pre-existing damage in your documentation and communication.
Provide the customer with a clear written summary of what was done with their property and any ongoing recommendations.
closeout_and_documentation.voice_considerations: End professionally and confidently. Good closeout reduces callbacks and builds long-term trust.

project_manager_timeline.description: Day-by-day operational timeline from a Project Manager's perspective. Reflects realistic field flow rather than the linear job_functions order. Designed for scoping, daily check-ins, and managing techs.
project_manager_timeline.notes: Emergency calls and regular responses both start on 'Day 1'. Demo is usually scheduled for Day 2 or later after testing/approvals. Drying phase typically runs 3–5 days after demo and cleaning.

day1.name: Day 1 - Initial Response & Stabilization
day1.goals: Stop the loss, remove bulk water, stabilize the environment, and get drying started.
key_tasks: Initial moisture readings and psychrometrics taken

day2.name: Day 2 - Demo & Post-Demo Work
day2.goals: Remove unsalvageable materials and prepare the structure for effective drying.

monitoring.name: Days 3–5+ - Active Drying & Monitoring
monitoring.goals: Maintain proper drying conditions until all materials meet dry standards.
key_tasks: Daily monitoring of equipment performance (grains removed)
Consistent moisture readings in same locations
monitoring.typical_duration: 3 to 5 days after demo/cleaning (can be longer on complex jobs)

final.name: Final Day - Verification & Closeout
final.goals: Confirm the structure is dry and properly documented before teardown.
key_tasks: No musty odors or hidden moisture

mindset: Have a good time at work, don't take life too seriously. Science and math is cool, but we're not doing rocket surgery out here.
Don't yell at people, it could trigger as asthma attack.
Teamwork metrics: Humility, Hunger for Success, and People Smarts.
